title: Coloured pulp with natural pigments
subtitle: Tinting handmade paper at the pulp stage using plant-based colourants
excerpt: Adding colour to the pulp before sheet forming produces paper that is dyed through its whole thickness, the colour does not rub off, fade at cut edges, or migrate with moisture. This tutorial uses natural plant pigments (turmeric, madder, and indigo) mixed with the pulp slurry, covering the amounts, lightfastness differences, and mordanting options.
sourceNotes: Helen Hiebert, Papermaking with Plants (1998, Storey), chapter on colouring pulp. Lightfastness data for turmeric vs. madder vs. indigo follows standard craft-dyeing references: Jenny Balfour-Paul, Indigo (2000, British Museum Press) for indigo permanence.

glossaryTerms.definition: The resistance of a colourant to fading when exposed to light. Rated on a Blue Wool Scale (1-8) or similar: 1 fades quickly, 8 is highly permanent. For paper intended to be displayed, choose colourants rated 4 or above.
A chemical additive that helps pigment particles bond to paper fibres in the vat rather than washing out with the drain water. Common retention agents include alum and cationic starch.

content.text: Colouring at the pulp stage is more permanent than colouring the finished sheet, because the pigment is embedded in the fibres rather than sitting on the surface. The limitation is
: some natural pigments fade quickly, and the batch sections below give the relevant guidance. A
is optional but greatly reduces the amount of pigment you need and the amount that washes out into the drain.

Add 2-4 teaspoons of ground turmeric to the blended pulp before pouring into the vat. Stir well. Turmeric gives a bright yellow to orange-yellow paper. Lightfastness is low (Blue Wool Scale 1-2): turmeric paper fades noticeably within months if exposed to daylight. Use for interior project paper, envelopes, or paper not intended for long-term display.

Madder root powder gives warm pink to terracotta tones. Soak 20 g in 500 ml water and simmer for 20 minutes. Cool to room temperature, then strain the liquid into the vat and add the pulp. Mordant the pulp with alum (5 g of potash alum dissolved in 500 ml water, added to the vat before the madder liquid) to improve colour take-up and lightfastness. Mordanted madder on cotton pulp has moderate lightfastness (Blue Wool Scale 4).

Indigo pigment powder (not dye extract) added to the vat gives blue paper with high lightfastness (Blue Wool Scale 6-7). Use 1-3 g of pre-reduced indigo powder added directly to the pulp slurry. Indigo is a vat dye and needs to be in its reduced (leuco) form to bond with fibres, commercial pre-reduced indigo powder is the safest choice for home studio use. The resulting sheets range from pale denim blue to deep navy depending on concentration.

Pull sheets in the normal way. Note that coloured pulp drains slightly differently to natural pulp: heavily pigmented vats may clog the mould mesh faster, so rinse the mould after every four or five sheets. The first sheet or two after adding pigment are often the most vivid; subsequent sheets in the same vat will be slightly lighter as the pigment is consumed. Keep back a small amount of the concentrated pigment liquid to top up the vat if the colour fades.
